data.title: M-Tron Industries: A Hidden RF Player Behind Missile Systems, Fairly Valued
data.description:
I am always looking to add new names to my aerospace and defense coverage. I already cover over 130 aerospace and defense stocks, but I believe there are still many more names that are worthy of further investigation for investment. A reader tipped M-Tron Industries (MPTI) as a name following the company’s price movement post-earnings. In this report, I discuss the company’s activities, the most recent results and I explain why I believe the stock is a hold despite its growth prospects but still is very close to a buy rating.

M-Tron Industries Is Niche Radio Frequency Component Manufacturer

M-Tron is a small but important provider of high-reliability RF (Radio Frequency) component manufacturer focused on frequency and spectrum solutions. You can see this as the timing and signal integrity backbone of advanced electronic systems. The company designs filters, oscillators, crystal resonators, RF/microwave assemblies and high-power amplifiers for the aerospace and defense, space & satellite communication and communication & data infrastructure markets.

The company Is not a high-volume player but provides solutions when in high-spec and high-reliability applications such as electronic warfare, radar, space systems and guided munitions. That also means that the company is positioned extremely well for missile, radar and drone proliferation with deep vertical integration as the company stretches from material science to design to manufacturing, testing and integration. M-Tron is active in the critical RF components layer placing it as a Tier 2/2.5 supplier.

As demand is increasing, M-Tron faces a ramp up challenge that it has to execute but once executed successfully there should be substantial margin upside. M-Tron faces some risk in the sense that it has customer and integration overlap with other companies. Whereas M-Tron is active in the component layer offering high customization options, there are players such as Qorvo and Mercury Systems which are active in the semiconductor and subsystem layer. From technological perspective Qorvo is a potential competitor and from system architecture level Mercury Systems is a potential competitor. This all revolves around vertical integration that could mean that one of the companies venturing into respective spaces.

So, M-Tron has to remain competitive, meaning its needs to remain on the technological frontier and also explore vertical integration opportunities and capacity expansions. In that light, the company recently announced a rights offering which could result in gross proceeds of $42 million.

Double Digit Growth For M-Tron But With Margin Pressure

In the fourth quarter, sales grew 11.2% to $14.2 million driven by strong volumes on defense programs as well as avionics. Gross margin were down 80 basis points showing a moderate headwind from tariffs. Net income grew 60.1% to $3.4 million while adjusted EBITDA grew 46.8% to $4.5 million marking a strong margin improvement from 23.9% to 31.5%. However, we note that the company had previously reserved around $0.86 million in bonuses contingent to performance milestones being met. Those milestones were not met, and management reversed the bonus providing a tailwind to earnings in Q4. Adjusted for this, the EBITDA margins would be 25.5% showing a more moderated but still meaningful step up in EBITDA margins.

For the full year, revenues increased 11% to $54.4 million with gross margins declining from 46.2% to 44.4% driven by tariffs and product mix. Net income rose 10.6% to $8.45 million while adjusted EBITDA grew 12.9% to $12.582. Adjusted EBITDA margins improved from 22.7% to 23.1%. However, last year’s figures included 3% of sales in bonuses. Adjusting for that, the margins in 2024 would have been 25.7%. So, we do see some downward pressure on the margins driven by production ramp ups and tariff headwinds.

M-Tron Industries Stock Is Fairly Valued

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

M-Tron definitely has growth prospects. I believe that sales will grow 10% annually through 2028 with 14.8% growth in EBITDA and 16.4% growth in free cash flow. What I also do like about M-Tron Industries is the fact that it has no bank debt and its capital raise through a right of purchase announced in March leaves the company with a cash position that it can use to grow inorganically. Since the company has no debt, it means there is no balance sheet risk and the only risk faced is execution risk on program ramp ups. However, from valuation perspective we see that while the company is undervalued against the aerospace and defense peers it is fairly valued against its own median EV/EBITDA. So, for now I am assigning a hold rating. So, it is more a speculative opportunity rather than a name with a buy conviction. We do note that for some investors his may also look like an appealing buy and that would not necessarily be wrong. The scoring system is automated, so we score a stock on several metrics and the score that came out for M-Tron Industries was less than 5% off from a buy rating. So, it is not an unattractive stock. It just doesn’t meet the buy threshold by a narrow margin.

I expect that margins and sales will continue to rise with free cash flow continuing to improve strongly. Cash conversion is expected to remain strong on acceptable CapEx levels. Since the company has no debt leverage is not a concern and with proceeds from warrant exercises and capital raises the net cash position is comfortable. What we do note is that M-Tron has in some way or form make use of the cash it raises else it is just cash sitting on the balance sheet for which EPS have been diluted.

Emphasizing The Importance Of Signal And Time Integrity

While I do not believe that the stock is a buy at this point in time, I do believe it is important to note that the company does play an important role in the sense that it is positioned as a company with a product portfolio that is in demand for applications where failure is not an option. Quite often the companies that are gaining investor attention are the ones that build the fancy fighter jets and missiles, but behind that communication, time and signal integrity plays an important role and that is where M-Tron plays an important role.

In the days that I still found myself in lecture rooms for my Aerospace Engineering degree, I followed a module called Applied Numerical Analysis and Computational Modeling. As an example of the importance of these rather boring parts of the curriculum my lecturer provided an example of how residual time errors due to rounding accumulated over time inside the timer of a missile and actually would lead to missiles missing their targets.

Years later I found an example of a missile failure due to integer time values being converted to floating point leading to a cumulating rounding error over time. This was the Patriot missile failure in 1991 in Dhahran when a Patriot missile failed to intercept a Scud missile leading to 28 casualties. The internal timer had cumulated a time residual error of 0.34 seconds which multiplied by the speed of the a Scud missile meant that the tracking of the Scud missile by the Patriot missile was off by around 600 meters. Whether my lecturer had this real-world example in mind is something I do not know, but it does the importance of signal and time integrity.

Conclusion: Strong Prospects But Valuation Is Fair

I do believe that M-Tron has appealing growth prospects with growth in electronic warfare applications as well as missile defense and drones and growth in avionics markets. However, from valuation perspective I do believe that the stock is more or less fairly valued as there is no strong reason to believe that the company will trade in line with aerospace and defense peers any time soon. As a result, I am initiating coverage with a hold rating.

data.title: General Motors (GM) Stock Analysis: Why The Quant System Says "Strong Buy" | 2-Minute Analysis
data.description:
Follow General Motors on Seeking Alpha!

Learn more about Seeking Alpha Quant Ratings

Get Started With Seeking Alpha Premium Now

This video's transcript was generated by a third party. It is not curated or reviewed and is provided for convenience and information purposes only. The accuracy and completeness of the transcript are not guaranteed.

Welcome to 2-Minute Analysis. Our goal is to not only entertain but provide value and insights about the investments you care about. So let's throw two minutes on the clock and dive in.

Today we're looking at General Motors, ticker symbol GM. Kicking things off here with the Quant Rating system, it is currently a STRONG BUY rating on the stock. And we're going to dive into its metrics here in just a moment. Jumping over to the Seeking Alpha analysts, they are currently a BUY in aggregate on the stock, and that's from eight analysts providing their coverage in the last 90 days.

And lastly, Wall Street currently has a BUY rating on this stock as well, and that's 27 analysts across the street providing their coverage in the last 90 days. To learn more about how the Seeking Alpha Quant system and Seeking Alpha analysts outperform the market, visit the link in the description of this video.

Now let's dive deeper. This is a $65.97 billion market capitalization company found within the consumer discretionary sector and the automobile manufacturers industry.

Jumping into the valuation grade, it is currently a B-. We're going to kick things off here with the price-to-cash flow, which you currently see is 2.46 compared to the sector at 9.35. And that forward price-to-book is 0.99 compared to the sector at 2.15. Metrics looking pretty steady and healthy there.

Let's jump over to the growth grade, it is currently an A-. You'll notice here the EPS GAAP growth forward is currently 27.25% compared to the sector at just 10.19%. Also that operating cash flow growth year over year is 33.47% compared to the sector, which is only at 2.16%.

Jumping into the profitability grade, currently an A+. A couple things to highlight here. First off, cash from operations is $26.87 billion compared to the sector at just $327.1 million. Cash per share for this company is $23.17 compared to the sector at just $2.76. And lastly, net income margin is 1.46% compared to the sector at 3.98%.

Jumping into the momentum grade, it is an A grade. And that's because of the one-year price performance, this stock is up 54.62% while the sector is down 4.98%. Although more recently, the three-month price performance is more in line with the sector.

Jumping into the revisions, that's an A- grade. That is 19 up revisions and four down revisions for earnings per share over the last three months, and eight up revisions and nine down revisions for revenue numbers over the last three months.

Now General Motors does pay a small dividend yield of 0.99%. And that is, if we go back into the history context here, a company that cut its dividend during the COVID era and has recently reinstated the company's dividend over the last three years. However, looking into the yield as well, it is trailing the yield compared to its sector peers. So something that investors should keep in mind when they’re looking at General Motors stock.

Now come on over to Seeking Alpha, go ahead and follow General Motors, put it into your Seeking Alpha portfolio, get all the free breaking news alerts about this company. And that's going to wrap it up for this episode. If you have a ticker you want us to cover, add it to the comment section below. And don't forget to follow Seeking Alpha so you get notified when the next 2-Minute Analysis gets published.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Content is presented for informational purposes only and may be superseded by future events. data.title: Realty Income Remains In Play As Growth Is Set To Continue
data.description:
One of the most interesting REITs out there today has got to be Realty Income (O). With a market capitalization of $59.93 billion and an enterprise value of $88.98 billion, it is not the largest REIT. But it's certainly up there. Over the years, management has succeeded in steadily growing the company. They have done this without taking on too much in the way of risk. On top of this, the company continues to pay out a hefty yield that is superior to what most other similar firms boast. And as time progresses, the company continues to make investments in growth opportunities, including a recently announced joint venture that will bring its significant amounts of cheap capital.

In my last article about the firm, published in late December of last year, I decided to reaffirm it as a ‘buy’ candidate. This was in spite of the fact that the stock had underperformed the market leading up to that point. My decision to keep it rated that seems to have worked out so far. Shares are up 8.1% while the S&P 500 is down 5.4%. Long term, I do expect continued outperformance. Because of this, I firmly believe that maintaining it as a ‘buy’ candidate makes sense.

A great business at a decent price

From a purely fundamental standpoint, the only new data that has come out regarding Realty Income covers through the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Because of how recently that data came out, it would probably be wise to dig into that first. During that time, revenue for the enterprise came in at $1.49 billion. That was up from the $1.34 billion generated the previous year. This increase in revenue is intriguing because the actual number of properties that the company has ownership over fell from 15,621 to 15,511. However, what the company did was focused on larger properties that would generate more in the way of revenue for it. Even as the property count declined, leasable square feet for the business increased from 339.4 million to 355 million.

Some of the company's revenue improvement came from other miscellaneous sources that rose from $60.6 million to $88.4 million. But rental income increased more, growing from $1.28 billion to $1.40 billion. Most of that was attributable to the new properties that the company added to its portfolio, with those contributing $152.8 million for the quarter. There was also some improvement in same store rental revenue from $1.13 billion to $1.15 billion. Rent escalations helped here. But also helpful, almost certainly, was the fact that occupancy for the company inched higher from 98.7% to 98.9%.

As revenue for the company increased, profitability rose as well. I maintain that net profits really aren't terribly relevant in this space. But it is nice to see that they grew from $199.6 million to $296.1 million. Operating cash flow jumped from $972 million to $1.20 billion. And if we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $937.4 million to $1.19 billion. FFO, or funds from operations, expanded from $897.9 million to $985.9 million. And on an adjusted basis, the rise was from $921.9 million to $996.7 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business grew from $1.22 billion to $1.34 billion.

The chart above shows financial results for 2025 compared to 2024. Revenue, profits, and cash flows, all increased during this time. What's more, the firm looks set to continue expanding through 2026. Management actually provides a calculation for annualized EBITDA. They do this because of how rapidly they are adding new assets. That metric actually totaled $5.41 billion as of the end of the latest quarter. That's comfortably above the $5.23 billion in EBITDA that the company generated for all of 2025.

To put in perspective what I mean here, management made total investments in 2025 of $6.30 billion, $6.22 billion of which was its pro rata share. This involved 380 properties. During the year, however, the company sold off 425 properties, bringing in $744 million. That huge disparity might seem concerning at first glance. But these were lower value properties and management still succeeded in booking a gain on them of $177.6 million. For 2026, the firm expects to make investments totaling about $8 billion. This should hope to grow adjusted FFO per share from $4.28 in 2025 to between $4.38 and $4.42. Using the midpoint there, that would give us a reading of $3.99 billion. The actual number could end up coming in higher if management continues to issue a lot of stock throughout the year. Last year, for instance, it brought in $2.36 billion from share issuances.

If we use instead the results of the business say in 2024 and 2025, we can see in the chart above how shares are priced right now. Normally, I would consider this to be a fair value prospect. But for high quality industry leaders with stable business models, I am quite a bit more flexible. In the chart below, I took two of these metrics and stacked it up against five other similar enterprises. It is true that, on a price to operating cash flow basis, four of the five enterprises are cheaper than it is. But when it comes to the EV to EBITDA approach, only one of them ended up being cheaper.

This can be chalked up to the fact that management has been principled when it comes to overall leverage. Despite significant investment volumes, it has maintained a net leverage ratio of 5.43. As the chart below illustrates, only one of the five institutions that I compared it to is better off than it is in this regard. All of the others, meanwhile, are worse. This means that, in relation to other similar enterprises, Realty Income has a lower risk profile. And if anything, this indicates to me that the company is worthy of a bit of a premium anyways.

One thing I do know is that, because of its strong financial position, Realty Income is able to pay out a significant amount of capital to shareholders each year. In 2025, the firm made $2.92 billion worth of distributions to common shareholders. That helps to explain why its yield is 5.15%. In the chart below, you can see that this is superior to all but one of the institutions that I stacked it up against.

In addition to being attractive in relation to other companies that are similar to it, Realty Income also has a lot of fundamental things going for it. Right now, management estimates that the real estate asset value on US and European company balance sheets is about $14 trillion. Just opens the door for significant asset purchases as time goes on. And in all honesty, this number should only grow from here. For instance, the data center asset category is worth only about $500 billion today. This is a high growth part of the economy, expected to see annual revenue expand from $496 billion in 2023 to $2 trillion at the end of the decade. Companies that operate in this industry could benefit from additional capital. So the prospect of sale leaseback transactions is tantalizing to say the least.

Management has already proven itself capable of allocating capital in order to grow. From 2019 through 2025, the company deployed an estimated $69 billion of capital. This was made possible thanks to $75 billion of average annual sourcing volume during that window of time. This has included smaller properties. But it has included larger ones as well. Examples would be hyperscale data center investments amounting to $800 million, 7-Eleven locations worth $770 million, and $1.75 billion dedicated to the US gaming market, $950 million of which involved the Bellagio Las Vegas. Of course, the biggest investments have been of other players in the space, such as its acquisition of VEREIT in 2021 in exchange for $18 billion, and its $10 billion purchase of Spirit Realty in 2024.

Management seems to be incredibly interested in private capital. In fact, through 2025, the company brought in total commitments amounting to $1.5 billion. Considering that we are talking about an $18.8 trillion private commercial real estate market in the US alone, this is just a drop in the bucket in the grand scheme of things. But the newest development from management came on March 19th when the company announced that it has struck a deal for a junior venture with Apollo (APO). According to the terms of that agreement, Apollo will be investing $1 billion in exchange for a 49% equity interest in a portfolio of properties owned by Realty Income.

This will involve around 500 retail properties with annualized base rent of $140 million. Examples of major tenants for these assets include Dollar General (DG), CVS Health Corporation (CVS), BJ’s Wholesale Club (BJ), Family Dollar, and The Kroger Co. (KR). Those collectively account for 22.9% of the annualized base rent associated with those assets. And when it comes to industry exposure, we have 41.3% of income coming from the five largest industries, with dollar stores coming in at the top at 9.9%, followed by the 8.3% that comes from quick service restaurants.

We won't know the full impact that this joint venture will have on the company. But we do know that it brings in $1 billion of cash, in exchange for equity ownership, plus we are looking at another $500 million associated with debt. I am not a believer in the weighted average cost of capital. And that is simply because there is no such thing as a true and measurable cost of equity. But as the image below illustrates, using management's own estimates, the implied cost of capital for this joint venture will be about 6.2%. That compares to the 7% to 9.6% range that Realty Income as a whole has calculated for itself. This is a nice improvement, and will give the company fuel with which to grow. We should expect more transactions like this as time goes on. That is especially true with assets that are no longer core to the company's long term strategy.

Takeaway

As things stand, I believe that the market continues to underestimate Realty Income. Management seems to be doing a remarkable job. Leverage is low and the yield is high. Shares might not be the cheapest, but they certainly aren't expensive in relation to other similar enterprises. Add on top of this growth prospects like those that I highlighted throughout this article, and it seems clear to me that Realty Income is attractive enough to justify a ‘buy’ rating.

data.title: FRA: NAV Should Continue To Erode If Distribution Isn't Cut (Downgrade)
data.description:
Overview

We are in one of the most unique market environments of the last decade. Technology stocks have sold off because of the rising threat of AI and when the indices experience a period of volatility, income-focused funds tend to serve as a hedge. However, the higher interest rate environment has also put pressure on the debt market and caused securities to decline in value. Since the BlackRock Floating Rate Income Strategies Fund (FRA) provides exposure to a diverse range of debt securities, FRA has struggled to regain any positive momentum over the last few quarters.

When I previously covered FRA, I issued a hold rating due to the threat of defaults and limited growth potential at the time. Since my last coverage, the fund has fallen by more than 16.4% and despite its high yield of 13.5%, the fund has severely underperformed the S&P 500. Due to the pullback in share price, FRA now trades at a discount to NAV of 5%. For reference, FRA has traded at an average discount to NAV of 3.3% over the last five year period. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that FRA continues to fall to the more attractive end of its historical price to NAV range. Despite this, I do not believe that it is a good time to accumulate shares as additional downside risks remain.

Since my last coverage, the fund has released an updated annual report for the 2025 period. The report indicates that the fund is still actively paying out more than twice of what it earns. As a result, I expect the NAV will continue to deteriorate over the next twelve months unless management decides to cut the distributions. However, the decision to keep payouts unchanged may be to help artificially deflate the fund's discount to NAV valuation. Once the discount tightens, management may then reduce payouts.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, FRA has total managed assets of $510M that are spread across 464 different positions. Unlike funds that invest in a pool of equities and rely on earnings growth of its positions, FRA's approach is to invest into debt securities. That means the success of FRA is dependent on how much interest income it can collect, which is directly tied to a borrower's ability to repay their loans. Debt investments are typically easier to gauge because a borrower's ability to repay a loan can be determined through strong underwriting. So even if market indices are declining and a business isn't growing, FRA collects income so long as borrowers keep up with their debt maintenance.

The fund has a management fee of 0.75% of managed assets, which is high but understandable given the active management. Unlike funds that track an index, FRA doesn't have any set rebalancing schedules in place. Therefore, investors are entirely relying on management's ability to find quality opportunities that can survive headwinds. As the fund's name implies, FRA invests at least 80% of its assets into securities that are structured on a floating rate basis. So in a higher interest rate environment, FRA can collect greater interest income from these securities.

Expanding on the structure, FRA also provides an efficient allocation across different industries. FRA is currently most heavily allocated to technology securities at 17.92% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to capital goods and consumer cyclicals, which accounts for 17.80% and 17.14% of assets. This diversity helps reduce any sort of concentration risks.

However, the market is selling off technology companies due to the rising threats of AI. Investors are now cautious that the moat for software companies has effectively been eliminated because of the cost-efficiency, ease of use, and rapid expansion of the capabilities of existing AI tools. Since FRA has some exposure to technology companies, the portfolio may be negatively impacted by the selloff. However, it is difficult to get transparency into the technology positions within the fund so it's something to be cautious about.

When looking at the positions within FRA, we can see there's exposure to a mix of public and private entities. For instance, the largest individual holding is the Invesco Senior Loan ETF (BKLN) at 2.03%. However, this is followed by many other private entities, including Alliant Holdings, Beignet Investor, USI Inc, and xAI Corp, just to name a few. Therefore, FRA is an interesting choice for investors that want diversity beyond what's publicly available for shareholders.

Valuation Risks

Although the fund trades at what appears to be an attractive discount to NAV valuation, I believe there is additional downside risk that investors must consider. Despite the fund initiating several interest rate cuts throughout the 2025 period, the federal funds rate still remains near the higher end of its ten-year range. Even during the latest Fed meeting, it was decided to leave interest rates unchanged. While a high interest rate is technically supposed to lead to higher interest income, it has actually put pressure on borrowers that are already operating with a tight spread.

Since rates have remained elevated for so long, this has led to an increased rate of defaults across the debt markets. The U.S now accounts for 85% of global defaults on a YTD basis. If interest rates do not decline over the next twelve months, I believe that defaults can continue to rise. We still have to deal with geopolitical conflicts, an uptick in employment, and high inflationary data that may cause the Fed to remain on the sidelines to see how things play out. Going forward, S&P Global estimates that the rate of defaults can increase to 4.75% by the end of 2026.

By reviewing the fund's latest annual report for 2025, we can gather additional insights about the weakness of the portfolio. Net investment income amounted to $0.91 per share, while net realized losses landed at $0.21 per share. Looking back over the last few years, we can see how net investment income has declined from the prior high of $1.24 per share back in 2023. As the fund deals with lower interest rates and rising defaults, net investment income has declined. The main issue is that net investment income doesn't support distributions by itself, so the fund is reliant on capturing positive net realized gains to bridge the gap in earnings.

However, a high interest rate environment means that there are few borrowers seeking debt financing while the cost of debt is elevated. This means that management has struggled to find new investments to grow its base assets. A higher interest rate also causes some of its positions to decline in value, which makes it difficult to capture positive net realized gains. This dynamic has led to FRA's underlying NAV deteriorating over the last few years. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $13.85 per share
  • 2022: $12.81 per share
  • 2023: $13.26 per share
  • 2024: $12.89 per share
  • 2025: $12.10 per share

These issues are further exacerbated by the fact that FRA utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify its earnings when possible. The use of effective leverage now amounts to 22.75% of assets, which is equivalent to ~$121M. While this is a moderate use of leverage, it can still increase the risk of the fund. Leverage can amplify downside movements if allocated to securities that don't perform as anticipated. Leverage is also one of the reasons why the fund's net investment income may be declining, since a greater portion of its assets need to be allocated towards interest expenses.

Generous Dividend Is Eroding NAV

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.1238 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 13.5%. If we multiply this payout by twelve months, we get an estimated annual distribution of $1.4856 per share if we assume that FRA will not adjust the payouts. Looking at the payout history over the last ten years below, we can see that FRA's distributions now sit at their highest levels. However, the fund continues to struggle generating sufficient earnings that can cover the payouts, which directly leads to NAV erosion over time.

For reference, the fund reported total earnings of $0.70 per share for 2025, which is well below the level it needs to be. In fact, the fund has only generated distributions sufficient earnings in one of the last five years. Therefore, I would argue that it is crucial for distributions to be substantially reduced in order to preserve the health of its NAV. With a yield of 13.5%, the fund would be able to reduce payouts by 25% and still offer a starting dividend yield around ~10.1%, which is still very competitive amongst the high yield landscape.

As long as the fund continues to pay out more than it earned, the underlying NAV will erode. Therefore, the high yield is simply not worth the risk. What good is a 13.5% yield if the share price were to decline by 10%? Therefore, it becomes hard to recommend a position in FRA for investors that want to collect a reliable source of dividend income and preserve capital over time. Even in a scenario where interest rates trend downward over the next twelve months, this will translate to lower interest income that can be collected from its securities.

However, I partially believe that management may have artificially inflated the distributions as a way to shrink the fund's discount to NAV valuation. At the fund's deepest level, FRA once traded at a discount to NAV around 13% at the tail end of 2022, which is coincidentally when the fund started to raise the distributions. Once the discount to NAV is eliminated, management may then decide to substantially reduce the payouts. If this happens, my outlook for the fund would improve.

Lastly, we get further reinforcement that FRA cannot support distributions when looking at the section19(a) notice. Approximately 52% of the distributions paid on a YTD basis were classified as return of capital, which is a sign that FRA is not generating enough income to support those payouts. While return of capital distributions are tax-efficient, they directly reduce the NAV and can make the issue of not generating enough income worse.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am downgrading my rating to a sell at this time. The fund continues to sell out more than it earns and this is directly impacting the rate of NAV erosion. In a scenario where interest rates don't trend lower over the next twelve months, FRA may be impacted by rising defaults and lower earnings. Furthermore, a high cost of debt means that management has fewer investment opportunities available to grow its base assets and net investment income. FRA would improve from a substantial dividend cut to help slow the rate of NAV deterioration and improve internal cash flows.

data.title: CoreWeave: Why I Am Reiterating A Buy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

CoreWeave (CRWV) is still one of my best ways to play out the infrastructure buildout. The difference today is that I do not have to lean on vague optimism. And I say this with reference to the 4Q 2025 Earnings report. The company just guided a $12 to $13Bn in revenue. And they also announced that they have a $66.8Bn revenue backlog. That backlog is not some sort of a hope number. In fact it is tied to committed customer contracts, subject to delivery and service availability which is exactly why capacity execution matters. In my previous articles, I rated the company a Buy. And I still believe that there is more expected upside than downside. That is why I am reiterating CRWV with a Buy rating.

Now here is the fresh part that matters for this update on CRWV. I believe the market has been treating CRWV like a leveraged GPU landlord. And this is because the 1Q 2026 margins are guided to trough. Capex is also expected to explode. Perhaps this is where many investors are expected to panic. CRWV's model is beginning to get financed like contracted infrastructure. I say this in reference to the recent announcement of an $8.5Bn delayed draw term loan facility. In my opinion, this is a big signal. Because it tells me that institutional capital is willing to underwrite GPU backed assets when there is an associated customer contract. And to me that is the real derisking of the "how do they fund this?" kind of question.

In my last piece, I said that every time "...CoreWeave thinks it has built enough capacity, customer keep asking for more.." And today, that line still holds water. What has changed is that CRWV now has clearer targets for where this demand converts into revenue. The company is also targeting an exit of 2026 with between $17Bn and $19Bn. And in 2027, an exit of more than $30Bn. Of course that is not some small talk. It is actually a map.

Business Overview

For context, Coreweave is a purpose built AI cloud. It focuses on high density GPU clusters for training and inferencing. There is also a software stack that is needed to run those clusters reliably at scale. I would simply call it an "AI factories as a service." But with serious operational tooling behind it.

Now, lets get something across. CoreWeave is scaling real fast. At the end of 2025, CRWV reported more than 850MW of active power and about 3.1GW of contracted power. Also it added roughly 260MW of active power in Q4 alone. Now, I am saying this because AI infrastructure is turning into a power and people problem and not just a chip problem. According to McKinsey & Company, the rapid rise in AI driven power density is transforming data centers and demands form tens of MWs to hundreds and even GWs scale.

Growth Drivers

First and foremost, one of the growth drivers is simple conversion. A $66.85Bn revenue backlog gives CRWV a couple of years of visibility. But it still has to turn into delivered capacity and billed dollars. As per their 2026 revenue guidance of between $12Bn and $13Bn is telling me that the conversion is accelerating. The ARR targets in 2026 and 20027 make it clear what management thinks conversion looks like once the buildout catches up.

Secondly, there is attach layer. Which I think is underappreciated. And storage is the clean example because of the growing numbers. I note that the company surpassed $100Mn in ARR from storage. The attach rates were about 800% for large customers. What this means is that customers are not just renting GPUs. But they are also adopting the platform. Overtime, I believe that should support the long term margin framework.

In my previous article, I also spoke about the software angle. The Nvidia (NVDA) partnership expansion included the path to offer CRWV software more broadly. And this could become margin accretive if it turns into licensing or "software outside our data centers" revenue. It is early and not in the management guidance. But it is exactly the kind of upside that changes the multiple over time.

Lastly, I would say the ecosystem map. And this is because CRWV sits at a busy intersection. NVDA supplies the critical GPUs and has invested $2Bn at $87.20 a share as part of a deeper partnership. Hyperscalers and AI labs are demand concentrators. Enterprises are the next adoption wave. And the supply chain is power, cooling and skilled labor. As per McKinsey, AI data center growth is clear that power infrastructure and utility delivery can slow expansion in traditional data center hubs. And this is why I believe CRWV has the ability to contract power and deliver quickly. And that is part of the moat.

Valuation

Let me start with the honest part. CRWV is not cheap on sales multiples compared to the sector median. And if I look at the valuation grade below, that part is quite clear. Because FWD EV/Sales trade at 5.41x while the sector median trades at 2.82x, suggesting a 92% premium.

Now here is the part where I am still comfortable rating CRWV a Buy. The FWD EV/EBITDA trades at 9.11x while the sector median trades at 12.71x. This implies a 28.32% discount to the sector. Additionally, the company also trades at FWD Price/cashflows of 4.67x while the sector median stands at 16.40%. This suggests a 71% discount relative to the sector median. This is a much larger discount. In my opinion, the market is punishing the capex cycle but the FWD cashflows angle is starting to tell a different story.

When I compare CRWV to some of its peers, I note that NVDA trades at a much richer EV/EBITDA multiple that CRWV's FWD 9.11x. NVDA trades at 16.70x. But remember, NVDA is a different business with different margins and a different capital profile. Microsoft (MSFT) and Oracle (ORCL) trade with FWD non-GAAP P/E ratios in the low 20s [MSFT at 22.13x while ORCL at 19.75x] and FWD PEG [MSFT at 1.61x while ORCL sits at 0.97x]. Now I brought MSFT and ORCL because this is what mature platforms look like. Yet for CRWV, it is being valued like a business that is still trying to prove its steady state even though the company is guiding long term 25% to 30% adjusted operating margin. I believe this kind of mismatch is where the upside lives.

Key Risks

The biggest risk I see as of now is execution risk. And this is in reference to the backlog that they have. At the end of the day, it will depend with how fast the company is able to convert backlog into delivered, billable capacity. This means that if CRWV fails to convert, this could end up hurting revenues.

Final Thoughts

At the end of the day, CRWD is building into a demand environment that is widening by the day. The backlog is $66.8Bn and the attach story is real with story adoption already high among large customers. I believe CRWV's better days are yet to come. And that is why I am reiterating CoreWeave with a Buy rating.

data.title: FTHI: Actively Managed Buy-Write Strategy For Equity Income Investors
data.description:
The First Trust BuyWrite Income ETF (FTHI) is an actively managed exchange-traded fund designed to provide investors with broad market exposure across all market capitalizations while generating income through a strategic covered call options strategy on the S&P 500 Index (SPX). Given the fund’s objectives, FTHI can potentially provide investors with differentiated returns given the flexibility to invest across market caps and different regions. The fund has performed exceptionally well when compared to its two benchmark indices, the SPX and the Cboe S&P 500 BuyWrite Monthly Index, presenting an appealing case as an alternative investment strategy for index investors. I am recommending FTHI with a "Buy" rating.

Investment Thesis For FTHI

FTHI provides investors with diversified equity exposure across all market capitalizations, enabling the fund to optimize the portfolio in any market environment. While the fund generally invests in U.S. equities, the FTHI has the flexibility to invest in foreign companies through ADRs, allowing for the fund to invest in offshore opportunities that may hedge U.S. exposure if necessary.

Compared to its benchmarks, FTHI is significantly more concentrated, with 186 holdings that make up the portfolio strategy. Sector weights within FTHI widely vary from its benchmark, providing investors with greater diversification relative to the benchmark Indexes.

Sector weighting widely differs from reported weightings in December 2025, presenting the case for active management and derisking the portfolio. This may have led to the portfolio’s outperformance over the last 3-month period.

With the market undergoing significant volatility resulting from exogenous geopolitical risk, I believe active management presents an appealing approach to investing when compared to indexed strategies. A core rationale behind this is that opportunities may reside in sectors that are underrepresented in the Large-Cap Index that may be exploited by FTHI through strategic security selection. While sector over-weightings may suggest a more defensive approach with higher allocations to utilities, consumer defensive, industrials, and energy, these, in part, may be viewed as the new growth sectors through U.S. reindustrialization.

For example, utilities are set to invest $1.1 trillion through 2030 to expand and modernize the power infrastructure, a major initiative that will benefit sectors that are heavily weighted in the fund, including financials for financing projects, industrials for construction and component sourcing, and energy for load capacity. In its real estate sector allocation, FTHI more heavily weights data center REITs, supporting the theme for AI data center development and leasing.

Enhancing the strategy, FTHI actively manages a covered call strategy by selling S&P 500 Index call options to generate income for the fund. The fund will generally write call options that are at-the-money or slightly out-of-the-money with less than 1 year to expiration. The fund may also write call spreads to hedge risk with respect to the options sold. A call spread is the act of buying a call at a higher price with respect to the call sold to limit downside risk of the short-call strategy.

FTHI has presented an appealing dividend distribution with a rate of $2.06/share over the last twelve months, yielding 8.99%. The distribution rate has exhibited steady growth in recent years. Investors should note that a large proportion of the distribution has historically derived from return of capital, which is generally common across buy-write strategies. ROC provides investors with a deferred tax benefit on their distributions, lowering the cost basis over time. Once the cost basis reaches $0/share, excess ROC will be taxed as capital gains.

About First Trust BuyWrite Income ETF

FTHI was launched by First Trust Advisors on January 6, 2014, on the Nasdaq Exchange. The strategy charges a modest fee of 76bps, a relative premium over traditional ETF managers like Invesco (PBP) and iShares (IVVW). FTHI presents strong liquidity with $2b in net assets and $15.78mm in share value changing hands daily.

FTHI has performed exceptionally well compared to peer strategies, potentially suggesting that the active security selection provides investors with greater opportunity with respect to limiting equity exposure to the S&P 500 Index.

Risks Related To FTHI

FTHI employs a buy-write strategy while maintaining an active equity strategy, exposing investors to certain risks that should be considered before making a final investment decision. Investors may be exposed to manager risk, relying on the portfolio management team’s ability to appropriately select and weight stocks as well as execute its call strategy. The covered call strategy may limit the full upside potential of the portfolio, as the return of options sold is limited to the premium gained. FTHI’s equity strategy is loosely defined to provide the portfolio management team with substantial flexibility in asset allocation, potentially creating some uncertainty regarding future sector and company size allocations.

Final Thoughts

FTHI provides investors with an appealing investment opportunity, providing active security and sector rotation to generate capital growth with an options overlay to generate income.

FTHI has outperformed peer strategies along with periodic outperformance of its benchmark indexes, driven by strategic asset allocation efforts. Given its robust performance and income opportunity, I am recommending FTHI with a "Buy" rating.

data.title: Cal-Maine Foods: Time To Shine As Specialty And Prepared Foods Expand
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered Cal-Maine Foods (CALM), I talked about the company’s strong balance sheet and ongoing expansion into prepared foods that enhances their margin stability and helps diversify their income stream through a more predictable source, reiterating their Buy rating.

Following another solid quarter despite the cash flow dropping significantly as a result of egg prices normalizing, CALM remains a Buy, backed by their excellent financials, recent acquisitions boosting their pivot, and potential to benefit from long-term consumer trends.

Building On Their Strong Foundation

Cal-Maine reported a strong quarter, beating the market’s revenue and EPS guidance significantly and delivering a free cash flow of $353.2 million during the first 9 months of the fiscal year, which is about half of the level from a year ago, being explained by the significant drop in egg prices, further normalizing from the jump caused by bird flu outbreaks in recent years.

Meanwhile, CALM recently announced a $128.5 million acquisition to further develop their shell egg, egg and prepared foods platform. The fact that this was funded through the cash available on the balance sheet goes to show the kind of advantage the company’s strong balance sheet offers, as the company doesn’t have to rely on (or worry about) expensive debt and is in a very comfortable position to take advantage of opportunities using the cash.

This comes as their specialty eggs segment grew to 50.5% during Q3’FY26 of total shell egg sales and prepared foods grew to 9.5% of net sales, with these two now accounting for 52.9% of net sales, building their future higher-margin engine. This is the result of the company utilizing the influx of capital from the recent spike in eggs prices to significantly improve their balance sheet and strengthen their operations, and I believe the management took the right steps so far.

Financially, based on CALM’s latest report, we continue to see a major advantage, with the current assets covering their total liabilities more than four times, and even though the cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities dropped, we should keep in mind that they spent $299 million on acquisitions YTD, with the equity still growing.

Since the balance sheet allows them to be selective with their M&A activity and take advantage of opportunities, what’s left can be used for shareholder returns, with CALM returning ~$214.8 million in dividends alongside ~$101 million in buybacks YTD.

As they mention, the dividend represents about a third of their GAAP net income, with $350.8 million left under their buyback program. Combined, we’re talking about a dividend plus buyback yield of more than 8% - and that’s for 9 months of the fiscal year, although the first half of the year was inflated by high prices.

Time For The Pivot to Shine

Despite egg prices normalizing, CALM continues to prove their strength, and I believe this spike helped transform the business into a leaner and more profitable company going forward, which we have to take into account in the future, with interest income offering a solid safety net, for now. If anything, this drop in egg prices should be a great time for the company to prove their worth, placing their pivot in the spotlight and potentially attracting more trust from the market. The company’s CEO also highlighted this during their latest presentation:

Despite materially lower egg prices compared to the historic levels seen in the prior year, our diversified portfolio and operational execution enabled us to deliver solid results and maintain momentum. In our view, this reinforces the resilience of the model we are building that we expect will lead to more durable normalized earnings power.

However, despite egg consumption per capita in the US growing over time, we can see price pressure being reflected in this and still playing an important role, with lower disposable budgets also coming into play, leading to trade-offs and potentially affecting consumption, while even though we see an estimated recovery in 2026, it still remains significantly below the peak seen in 2019, before the pandemic-related issues and rate hikes began.

However, the ongoing conflict in Iran can also present a risk, as it can lead to significant inflationary pressure and weaken discretionary budgets even more. As we can see even from day one of the conflict, pump prices jumped significantly, pushing countries into various measures to try alleviating the shock, which can at the very least lead to a temporary slowdown - or worse.

I don’t expect this conflict to last for that long since a ground operation would not only last for and cost far more than anyone should be willing to pay given Iran’s weather, mountains, military and size that make it a true natural fortress on top of religious beliefs that fueled conflicts in the region for centuries, but that doesn’t mean the impact to the economy wouldn’t be significant.

Even if Marco Rubio’s and President Trump’s statements regarding a potential resolution soon are true, we saw warnings from the Fed and ECB about potential inflationary shocks, as fuel might still trade at a war premium even if the conflict is over fast given the damage that’s already been done to the region’s energy infrastructure following Iran’s counterattacks.

This can lead to higher-for-longer rate risks or even hikes in the near-term, which can be a negative for many valuations and can pressure CALM despite their strong financial position, with the stock proving recently how it can move together with the general market based on expectations surrounding this conflict and the Fed’s next moves.

Although this conflict hasn’t been reflected in egg prices so far, a period of prolonged pressure could also come as a boost, as we’re talking about something that has significant implications for the price of fuel, energy, fertilizers, and more. This can result in higher costs for CALM and their industry that would have to be reflected in their commodity, although corn and soybean for example are up only slightly following a spike in early March.

Meanwhile, a de-escalation can also be good for egg prices, as it can revert the flight to safety effect seen in March, when the market moved into the US Dollar as a way to deal with this uncertainty, which is also a reason why some commodities (e.g. precious metals) fell alongside stocks and treasuries (which pushed yields higher), potentially helping eggs as they’d be worth more dollars.

Still, this type of macro pressure and uncertainty highlight the company’s strategic importance, as it serves a vital role in the country’s food supply, while their recent investments and balance sheet improvements can help offset the underlying commodity's volatility. In fact, the solid cash position and the lack of debt can help them withstand a higher-for-longer rate environment as they could generate higher interest income that can help alleviate potential demand issues.

Regarding health trends, GLP-1, and potential MAHA initiatives, I’ll mention again that CALM can stand to benefit or at the very least not be affected that much, as eggs are ultimately a nutritious, affordable, and very versatile whole-food source of high-quality protein that fits these dietary shifts, while the egg and egg products market is expected to grow at a 5.1% CAGR between 2025 to 2032.

Risks Reiterated

If egg prices don’t recover, there’s also potential to see pressure on the company’s margins due to higher fuel/energy prices, even though the currently weak prices can allow them to offset some of this through solid demand.

Although the company has potential to be more resilient going forward given their investments and benefit from a higher-for-longer rate environment through higher interest income, they still depend on egg prices at the end of the day, and that kind of volatility is important to keep in mind, as even though they’re developing higher-margin or more predictable income streams, we’re still talking about a commodity company.

Valuation

For CALM’s DCF to Equity, given the company’s acquisitions, excellent financials providing interest income and gradual shift towards more reliable income, let’s assume a mid-cycle FCF of $250 million (which might still turn out to be conservative, but they’ll have to prove it to the market; reported ~$72 million in FCF in Q3’FY26), without taking into account net borrowings given their balance sheet.

I’ll assume they can grow this at a 4.5% CAGR over the first 5 years thanks to potential macro improvements, consumer trends, inflation, and investments into higher-margin operations, followed by a 3.25% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, going for a 9% discount rate to demand a better margin of safety given the fact that they're still a commodity stock at their core, with 47.414 million shares outstanding as of their Q3'FY26 report. Note that I’m not accounting for any buybacks boosting the per share results.

This gets us to an equity value of $4.40 billion, for an intrinsic value of $92.83 per share, which is above the current $83.25, leading to a solid margin of safety given their potential to deliver even better results as they keep developing and pivoting the business.

Conclusions

CALM is a Buy, backed by their excellent financial health, zero debt, strong cash flow and advancing pivot into more predictable income, with the recent avian flu helping them transform the business.

There are risks to keep in mind given the fact that they’re still a commodity company after all, but the risk-reward continues to be compelling at these levels, with the business being vital for the US and standing to benefit from long-term health-consciousness tailwinds.

“Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best Commodity Idea investment competition, which runs through May 31. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!”

data.title: Chagee: A Divergence In Short-Term And Long-Term Prospects
data.description:
I've kept Chagee Holdings Limited (CHA) as a "Hold"-rated name.

CHA's near-term financials will most probably be under pressure, judging by its results and guidance. But a turnaround in the mid-to-long term is possible with business restructuring initiatives and overseas penetration.

My December 1, 2025, initiation piece touched on the below-consensus 3Q25 metrics and an unexpected cash distribution.

4Q Bottom Line Was A Negative Surprise

The firm issued a press release revealing its latest performance on the morning of March 31.

CHA's Oct-Dec '25 earnings were 87% lower year-on-year at CNY0.49/share. According to S&P Capital IQ, that's also 68% short of the CNY1.54 consensus prediction.

I mentioned the Chinese beverage sector's "fierce rivalry" in the late-2025 write-up. This unfavorable environment continues to be a drag on topline.

The group's results disclosure highlighted a 26% drop in "Average monthly GMV per teahouse in Greater China" to CNY0.34 million for 4Q2025. It was explained at the analyst meeting that "we chose not to chase low-price traffic blindly."

BofA (BAC) published a report (not publicly available) on April 2 titled "Guming: Share Gainer With Good Visibility." BAC's research indicated that the average "revenue growth for China's top 5 drinks players" including Chagee, was +27.7% last year. CHA has ceded ground to competitors because of its prestige pricing. That's evidenced by its modest 4% turnover increase during the same period.

Separately, a shift in outlet ownership mix impacted profits.

CHA's call commentary suggested that it "converted some of our franchisee tea houses into owned ones in China." I can understand why the company wants to have greater operational oversight in challenging times like these.

The enterprise stated in its earnings release that "company-owned teahouse operating costs" were 131% higher at CNY377 million on YoY terms last quarter. In my view, this played a big role in the 16ppts net margin contraction from 4Q24's 19% to 4Q25's 3%.

FY26 Guide Is Underwhelming

The shares fell by 3.1% and 1.6% on Tuesday and Wednesday, respectively, post-results. I think investors were disappointed by the group's outlook, on top of the Q4 miss.

At the earnings call, management noted that "our goal is to keep revenue and profit broadly flat" for the current year. That's largely consistent with the sell-side's projection of a low-single-digit (+3%) rise in FY2026 net income as per S&P Capital IQ.

My take is that it will be difficult for CHA to do better than what it's targeting. This implies that short-term prospects are poor.

It opened 824 shops (excluding closures) in China for 2025. But the firm is only anticipating domestic net additions of 300+ this year.

Also, CHA has stated at the analyst briefing it's prioritizing "market share" over "profitability" in '26. You would assume that the enterprise is likely to invest substantially to protect its competitive position.

Business Realignment Potential Shouldn't Be Ignored

Looking beyond 2026, I've identified some financial improvement levers.

The firm acknowledged in its results announcement that it had a more moderate "cadence of new product launches" for 2H25. It's reasonable for me to expect a growth acceleration once CHA becomes more aggressive on portfolio expansion. As an example, half of its inactive members re-engaged with the brand in Dec '25 following the rollout of the "Signature Four Tea."

There's room to broaden the brand's positioning too. CHA is a favorite among office workers grabbing drinks after lunch. In May last year, management talked about "low-caffeine selections" that "captured increased afternoon and evening consumption occasions." I can imagine the company diversifying its lineup to attract more consumers in the future.

CHA's earnings briefing comments implied that there are plans for further cost-cutting. It disclosed specifically that "we've wrapped up Phase 1" of "a bigger long-term push to make the organization healthy." My opinion is that the group's profitability could recover over time as it becomes leaner. Analysts are forecasting a widening of its pre-tax margin from FY26's 16.4% to FY28's 17.5% (source: S&P Capital IQ).

International Runway Is Still Long

Non-Chinese operations were a bright spot in the prior year. The group's 4Q2025 foreign GMV of CNY0.37 billion represented an 85% YoY jump. Its overseas store network expanded by +121% to over 300 locations for FY25.

I'm impressed by the company's success in Vietnam last year. At the results meeting, CHA shared that its brand visibility rose to "second in the local tea category" shortly after penetrating this market.

The firm intends to venture into South Korea in Apr-Jun '26. That will provide support for its international net outlet opening target of 200+ in the full year.

CHA's proportion of GMV derived outside of China was just 5% for the final quarter of 2025. It's only present in seven international markets. I believe there's a lot of whitespace that the group can capture going forward.

My Closing Remarks

I'm rating this stock as "Neutral." There are divergent read-throughs from its recent financial release.

CHA is trading at a fair valuation based on the "Price-to-Earnings Growth" or "PEG" ratio.

I've calculated its PEG to be 0.92 times (5.9/6.4), or pretty close to the 1x benchmark. As per S&P Capital IQ, CHA's forward P/E and consensus FY26-28 net profit CAGR are 5.9x and +6.4%, respectively.

data.title: Hess Midstream: Iran Conflict Reduces Bakken Production Fears
data.description:
Shares of Hess Midstream (HESM) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing about 11% of their value (though the stock does pay an ~8% dividend yield). Shares were hit hard during the second half of 2025 as concerns grew about Chevron (CVX) reducing its drilling activity in the Bakken given its higher costs, raising fears about long-term production levels and cash flow even as near-term results are protected by contractual minimum volume commitments (“MVC”).

The recent rise in prices, driven by the Iran War, has lifted shares in the thinking that higher prices could spur more drilling. Since I rated HESM a "Strong Buy" in December, shares have gained 10%, and with questions about the future of the war and energy prices, now is a good time to revisit shares.

Hess Midstream struggled in the middle of last year amid concerns about long-term production in the Bakken region, given its higher-cost base than plays like the Permian. Indeed, Chevron is now operating just three rigs in the Bakken, which should essentially keep oil production flat through 2028, while gas production should increase modestly. We need to see prices above $70 to feel comfortable about long-term production trends, and much of last year, we fell below that level.

With prices having surged to about $100, Bakken drilling economics are now much more favorable. There is a tremendous amount of uncertainty around the Iran War and, in particular, how long flow through the Strait of Hormuz is impaired. The longer this goes on, the more likely prices are to stay high. Even after an eventual resolution, heightened geopolitical risk may keep prices a bit higher. Plus, in my view, the world has already lost over 300-350 million barrels (given a 10-12M daily shortfall) meaning the market is structurally tighter. Plus, curtailments that have occurred in the Middle East cannot immediately be reversed. There will be at least a 500-million-barrel drawdown of inventories in my view. Prices will need to stay higher for longer to incentivize production that brings inventory back to a normal level.

I am unconvinced we have seen a peak in prices, but even if we have, prices are likely to stay above $80 this year, which meaningfully improves the production profile of the Bakken. This is why HESM has reacted so well over the past month, even as its business does not take commodity price risk directly. As a reminder, 100% of its revenue is fee-based with annual increases, typically tied to inflation, to maintain margins.

However, higher production should increase throughput and thereby revenue, giving the company some second-derivative exposure to commodity prices. Now, HESM has a favorable long-term contract with CVX that runs through 2033, providing downside protection given minimum volume commitments. In 2026, MVCs protect about 95% of revenue. However, MVC coverage declines to 80% by 2028. Under its contract, once set, MVCs cannot be lowered in a year, and in the annual updating, I expect its 2028 MVC levels to rise somewhat.

While MVCs provide very certain near-term revenue, the drop-off in 2028 has raised concern about HESM being a melting ice cube. However, in a higher commodity price environment, this is much less likely to be the case, as Bakken production should be at least stable to current levels. My current base case is that production runs in the 200mboe/d range for the next few years. I do not necessarily expect a big surge in production from higher prices, but a $70-$100 WTI price is sufficient to make it attractive for CVX to sustain operations.

Importantly, HESM has shifted its strategy over the past year to maximize cash flow in a flattish production environment. In December, Hess Midstream provided long-term guidance, including 5+% distribution growth through 2028, which it can achieve even at MVC levels. Beyond this, it expects to generate $1 billion of excess cash flow, which can be used for buybacks as well as debt reduction as it now seeks to reduce leverage from its current 3x level. In 2026, the company should invest about $150 million, down roughly $100 million from 2025. Beyond this year, cap-ex will be closer to $75 million as HESM spends just to maintain its current footprint rather than expand. This lower cap-ex investment should facilitate higher free cash flow over the next two years.

These efforts to better manage spending are also paying dividends. In the company’s fourth quarter, reported back on February 2, Hess Midstream earned $0.72 per share, which beat estimates by $0.06 as revenue grew 2% to $404 million. In Q4, EBITDA was $309 million. This was up from $298 million last year thanks to an increase in margins to 83% from 81%. Revenue was up in Q4 even as gas gathering volumes fell about 1.5%, while processing volumes were down just under 1%. Crude volumes were down about 2%. This reflects the benefit of annual price increases, which further buoys revenue.

In 2026, HESM is targeting $850-$900 million of free cash flow with ~10% growth in 2027 and 2028. Guidance assumes EBITDA of ~$1.25 billion in 2026, which is flat from last year, as free cash flow improvement is entirely due to cap-ex reductions rather than underlying business growth. I view 2026 guidance as highly credible given revenue is fee-based and protected by MVCs. I do not see much near-term upside from the Iran conflict, as it will take time for that to impact production. Rather, I view this environment as helping HESM’s medium-term outlook.

It is worth noting that shares currently offer an 8% dividend yield. This payout is fully covered by free cash flow, and I expect at least $200 million of excess cash flow. In March, the company did an accelerated $60 million buyback, which included buying back $18 million of Chevron’s stake. Chevron holds a 37.8% stake after this sale, so its decision to be about 30% of the buyback was broadly in line with its existing stake. I expect CVX to broadly sell shares in buybacks in proportion to its existing ownership stake to maintain effective control and ~37%-38% ownership. I expect about $150-$200 million of buybacks this year. This is a bit slower than past years, given the goal of gradually reducing leverage.

Given all of these factors, I view HESM as very well positioned to grow its distribution by at least 5% over the next three years while bringing leverage closer to 2.75x, leaving it with one of the healthiest balance sheets in the space. I view fears of HESM being a melting ice cube as overdone and expect it will be able to continue dividend growth beyond 2028. Plus, I continue to believe CVX could at some point seek to acquire the rest of HESM that it does not own to simplify its own capital structure, though the timing of any such deal is uncertain. With an 8% yield and 5% growth, I see 13+% annual medium-term return potential and view fair value as closer to $45. As such, I maintain a "Strong Buy" on HESM even after its run and would use the pullback over the past couple of days to add shares.

data.title: There's A Storage Problem With AI Inference - And Western Digital Knows It
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In my last piece on Western Digital (WDC), I described the setup as a company on the cusp pf significant and long term growth. And this was majorly driven by AI, Cloud and next-gen HDD tech. I still believe so. And so far what has changed is that the setup has turned into proof.

Let us start with the demand signal. When I look at the Q2 2026 earnings report, I can comfortably say that the company was trying to communicate the quite part out loud. By this I mean that they have firm purchase orders with their top 7 customers through 2026. And I also note that they have longer commercial agreements that stretch into 2027 and even 2028. Now, is that normal for historically cyclical hardware company. I don't think so. In fact, I believe that is what one gets when their customers are hyperscalers building AI infrastructure on multi year build schedules. And the product sits in the must have layer of the stack.

For some context, let me zoom out a bit. AI is forcing one of the largest data center build outs in modern history. According to a report by SP Global, global data center power demand is expected to almost 2x by 2030. And this power demand is majorly driven by a surge in data center capacity. In another report, McKinsey estimates that global data center capacity is expected to compound at between 19% and 22% through to 2030. The report also adds that spending wave is a potentially a multi trillion by the end of the decade. And this is mainly driven by AI workloads. Now, that is the backdrop WDC is selling into.

Let me now zoom back into the economics. WDC is not winning because it is so special. It is winning because the product mix is moving to higher capacity nearline drives. And pricing per TB is stable to up. And cost per TB is also coming down. In the 2Q 2026 earnings call, the CEO talked about incremental gross margin which is expected to be about mid 70% range. And the cost per TB is down about 10% y/y. Already, I can see that WDC has the operating leverage that turns a goof cycle into a serious compounding window.

Finally, the multimodal angle is also important to mention. The CEO called out larger multimodal models being propelled by autonomous vehicles and robotics. Why is this important? Because multimodal workloads create bigger data sets, more logs and more retrieval layers and more stored history. Training of course gets the headlines. But inference is where the world starts generating and saving data at scale. When more data is created, it means more data is also stored. And HDDs remain the cheapest place to store massive cold and warm data in tiered architectures.

And this is why I am comfortable reiterating WDC as a Strong Buy. I mean since the first article, the stock has moved 346%. And when I look at the economics, fundamentals, TAM and demand visibility, everything keeps getting better.

Business Overview

Western Digital today is simpler than most people remember. The company completed the separation of its flash business into Sandisk (SNDK) in February 2025. And since then, WD's financials are presented with SNDK as discontinued operations. And WDC is effectively a standalone HDD company that is focused on nearline and cloud storage demand.

And the revenue mix tells me exactly where the oxygen is. In Q2 2026, WDC reported $3.017Bn in revenue and cloud represented about $2.673Bn of the total. Client and consumer were single digits of the revenue. And to me, this means one thing. That WDC is a cloud infrastructure supplier and cloud is where AI is getting built.

Now on the product side, WDC is scaling capacity through ePMR today. And also pushing UltraSMR adoption and ramping towards HAMR for the next step function in areal density. The company has shipped over 3.5Mn units of its latest generation ePMR products, offering up to 26TB CMR and 32TB UltraSMR capacities. To me, this means increase volume shipping.

When I look at the ecosystem map of WDC this is what I see. AI compute, high speed networking and storage are chained together. When compute layer scales, then data movement and data retention scales too. And as inference becomes more monetized, then the total cost of ownership becomes a standard for hyperscalers. HDD economics stay relevant because they lower the $/TB and can lower $/watt when design improves.

Growth Catalysts

Let us start with the sold out year. Yes, you heard that right. WDC is sold out for 2026 as noted by the CEO.

...we're pretty much sold out for calendar year '26. We have firm POs with our top 7 customers. And we've also established LTAs with two of them for calendar year '27 and one of them for calendar year '28. Obviously, these LTAs have a combination of volume of exabytes and price...

And this means that hyperscalers are not treating HDD buys as quartely decision. Instead they are treating them as capacity planning. And I believe this also lines up with the wider industry research. TrendForce has warned of severe nearline HDD shortage. And this shortage is tied to inference AI. Additionally AI generated data is also putting on data center storage infrastructure. And that is the kind of environment that supports a steady pricing and longer lead times. Which is exactly the setup that WDC is describing from the inside.

Then there is UltraSMR which I briefly mentioned. WDC crossed 50% of UltraSMR in its nearline portfolio and expects that mix to increase. Its a software based service and more UltraSMR mix can lift gross margin even if demand stays constant. This means that if demand grows, its a double win for WDC.

It is also important to mention that AI workloads are weird. They don't only need capacity. They also need throughput, predictable latency tiers and power efficiency. So that data centers do not turn into a utility problem. And this is why WDC highlighted technologies like High bandwidth drive and dual pivot designs aimed at higher bandwidth and IO performance. I also note that WDC is working on a power optimized HDD concept that uses less power. And if WDC can narrow the warm and cold storage gap, then it makes HDDS stickier deeper into the stack.

Valuation

In my previous write up, WDC looked like a discount stock on several traditional multiples. Even today after the rally, the stock still has a lot of upsides to offer. First and foremost, WDC trades at a FWD non-GAAP P/E of 30.38x while the sector median trades at 21.28x, suggesting a 42% premium. On the other side, the FWD EV/EBITDA stands at 19.87x while the sector median sits at 12.71x. I mean if I look at these two multiples, I note that WDC is trading at a premium.

But when I look at WDC on a growth adjusted basis, this is why I am comfortable reiterating my strong buy rating. WDC trades at FWD non-GAAP PEG of 0.68x while the sector median trades at 1.27x. This implies a 46.61% discount. So the market is paying up for the quality and visibility but it looks like it is underpricing the growth rate embedded in WDC's earnings. A PEG below 1.0x is not a magic wand. But in cyclical businesses it often signals that the market is still thinking peak while fundamentals are shifting higher for longer.

When I line up WDC up against peers, then the premium looks more like a re-rating of the top tier companies in the storage infrastructure space. Seagate Technology (STX) shows a similar FWD Non-GAAP P/E with a FWD non-GAAP PEG of 0.68x. NetApp (NTAP) has a FWD Non-GAAP PEG of 1.67x but has a lower FWD Non-GAAP P/E of 12.85x. Sandisk Corp (SNDK) on the other side has a FWD Non-GAAP PEG of 0.07x and a FWD non-GAAP P/E of 15.78x. So clearly, I note that WDC is getting valued more like a growth infrastructure supplier than a classic commodity hardware maker. And that is consistent with the company's own "AI era storage infrastructure" positioning.

Key Risks

The biggest risk I see for WDC is concentration and cycle timing. WDC is overwhelmingly exposed to cloud customers and cloud spending can pause. Anytime. I mean, if AI capex slows down or shifts timing, then WDC's revenue can wobble. And this means that if customers delay or pause their orders, this could also hurt WDC's revenues.

Final Thoughts

Well... this is what transformation looks like when it is real. Post the split from SNDK, WDC has become a cleaner story. Nearline HDD leader selling into AI driven cloud demand. And 2Q 2026 showed that revenue growth, gross margin expansion and real FCFs. Yet momentum is expected to continue. And that is the part that makes me lean in. The company has a sold out 2026 demand, long term agreements into 2027 and 2028. And a roadmap that is now confidently discussing 40TB qualification and a path to 100TB. And when I add UltraSMR to the mix, then the earnings power will start to look less cyclical and more compounding. At the end of the day, I am comfortable reiterating Western Digital as a Strong Buy because of these reasons.
